([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ภาษาไทยถิ่นเหนือ), also called **Kam Mueang** (Northern Thai: ᨣᩴᩤᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ กำเมือง), **Lanna** or **Tai Yuan**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) is the language spoken by the [Northern Thai people](/wiki/Northern_Thai_people "Northern Thai people") of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). It is a [Southwestern Tai language](/wiki/Southwestern_Tai_languages "Southwestern Tai languages"). The language has approximately six million speakers, most of whom live in [Northern Thailand](/wiki/Northern_Thailand "Northern Thailand"), with a smaller community of speakers in northwestern [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos").

(ᨤᩫ᩠ᨶᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ, [คน](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B8%84%E0%B8%99 "wikt:คน")[เมือง](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%B7%E0%B8%AD%E0%B8%87 "wikt:เมือง"), [[kʰon˧.mɯaŋ˧]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Northern_Thai "Help:IPA/Northern Thai") – literally "people of [Mueang](/wiki/Mueang "Mueang")" meaning "city dwellers"), Lanna, or Northern Thai. The language is also sometimes referred to as **Phayap** (พายัพ, Thai pronunciation: [[pʰāː.jáp]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")), "Northwestern (speech)".

(ᨣᩴᩤᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ กำเมือง, /kām mɯ̄aŋ/, literally "city language"; cf. [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): คำเมือง /kʰām mɯ̄aŋ/), or **phasa Lan Na** (ᨽᩣᩈᩣᩃ᩶ᩣ᩠ᨶᨶᩣ, ภาษาล้านนา /pʰāː.sǎː láːn nāː/, literally "the language of [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na")").
or **phasa nyon** (ພາສາຍວນ or ພາສາໂຍນ respectively, /pʰáː.sǎː ɲúan/ or /pʰáː.sǎː ɲóːn/ respectively, literally "the Tai Yuan language").

**This article contains Lanna text.** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(East_Asian)#Archaic_Hangul "Help:Multilingual support (East Asian)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Tai Tham script](/wiki/Tai_Tham_script "Tai Tham script").

**Northern Thai** ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ภาษาไทยถิ่นเหนือ), also called **Kam Mueang** (Northern Thai: ᨣᩴᩤᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ กำเมือง), **Lanna** or **Tai Yuan**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) is the language spoken by the [Northern Thai people](/wiki/Northern_Thai_people "Northern Thai people") of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). It is a [Southwestern Tai language](/wiki/Southwestern_Tai_languages "Southwestern Tai languages"). The language has approximately six million speakers, most of whom live in [Northern Thailand](/wiki/Northern_Thailand "Northern Thailand"), with a smaller community of speakers in northwestern [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos").

Speakers of this language generally consider the name "Tai Yuan" to be pejorative[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. They refer to themselves as **Khon Mueang** (ᨤᩫ᩠ᨶᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ, [คน](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B8%84%E0%B8%99 "wikt:คน")[เมือง](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B9%80%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%B7%E0%B8%AD%E0%B8%87 "wikt:เมือง"), [[kʰon˧.mɯaŋ˧]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Northern_Thai "Help:IPA/Northern Thai") – literally "people of [Mueang](/wiki/Mueang "Mueang")" meaning "city dwellers"), Lanna, or Northern Thai. The language is also sometimes referred to as **Phayap** (พายัพ, Thai pronunciation: [[pʰāː.jáp]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")), "Northwestern (speech)".

The term Yuan is still sometimes used for Northern Thai's distinctive [Tai Tham alphabet](/wiki/Tai_Tham_alphabet "Tai Tham alphabet"), which is closely related to the old Tai Lue alphabet and the Lao religious alphabets. The use of the **Tua Mueang**, as the traditional alphabet is known, is now largely limited to Buddhist temples, where many old sermon manuscripts are still in active use. There is no active production of literature in the traditional alphabet, and when used in writing standard Thai script is invariably used. The modern spoken form is called *Kam Mueang*. There is a resurgence of interest in writing it in the traditional way, but the modern pronunciation differs from that prescribed in spelling rules.[[3]](#cite_note-Natnapang-3)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

Further information: [Tai–Kadai languages](/wiki/Tai%E2%80%93Kadai_languages "Tai–Kadai languages"), [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages"), and [Tai peoples](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples")

Northern Thai is classified as one of the Chiang Saen branch, a model popularized by foundational linguists such as Fang-Kuei Li (1960) and Marvin Brown (1965), others being [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"), [Southern Thai](/wiki/Southern_Thai "Southern Thai") and numerous smaller languages, which together with the Northwestern Tai and Lao-Phutai languages, form the [Southwestern](/wiki/Southwestern_Tai_languages "Southwestern Tai languages") branch of [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages"). The Tai languages are a branch of the [Kra–Dai language family](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages"), which encompasses a large number of indigenous languages spoken in an arc from [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") and [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") south through Laos and Northern Vietnam to the Cambodian border.

From a purely genealogical standpoint, most linguists consider Northern Thai to be more closely related to [Central Thai](/wiki/Central_Thai "Central Thai") than to [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language") or [Isan](/wiki/Isan_language "Isan language"), but the language has been heavily influenced by both Lao and Central Thai throughout history. All Southwestern Tai languages form a coherent [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") of more or less mutually intelligible varieties, with few sharp dividing lines. However, this genealogical classification is increasingly contested by modern scholars. Critics argue that these groupings may reflect political "Thaification" and a drive for national unity rather than purely linguistic history. While the Chiang Saen group (including Central Thai, Northern Thai, and Southern Thai) is the standard model, it often masks the closer historical ties Northern Thai has with the Lao-Phutai branch.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Nevertheless, Northern Thai has today become closer to the Central Thai language, as Standard Thai is the principal language of education and government and spoken throughout Thailand.

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Names")]

The Northern Thai language has various names in Northern Thai, Thai, and other [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages").

* In Northern Thai, it is commonly called **kam mueang** (ᨣᩴᩤᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ กำเมือง, /kām mɯ̄aŋ/, literally "city language"; cf. [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): คำเมือง /kʰām mɯ̄aŋ/), or **phasa Lan Na** (ᨽᩣᩈᩣᩃ᩶ᩣ᩠ᨶᨶᩣ, ภาษาล้านนา /pʰāː.sǎː láːn nāː/, literally "the language of [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na")").
* In [Central Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Southern Thai](/wiki/Southern_Thai_language "Southern Thai language"), Northern Thai is known as *phasa thin phayap* (ภาษาถิ่นพายัพ /pʰāː.sǎː tʰìn pʰāː.jáp/, literally "the language of the northwestern region"), or *phasa thai thin nuea* (ภาษาไทยถิ่นเหนือ /pʰāː.sǎː tʰāj tʰìn nɯ̌a/, literally "the Thai language of the northern region", or colloquially it is known as *phasa nuea* (ภาษาเหนือ /pʰāː.sǎː nɯ̌a/, literally "the northern language").
* In [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"), it is known as **phasa nyuan** or **phasa nyon** (ພາສາຍວນ or ພາສາໂຍນ respectively, /pʰáː.sǎː ɲúan/ or /pʰáː.sǎː ɲóːn/ respectively, literally "the Tai Yuan language").
* In [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"), it is known as *kam yon* (ᦅᧄᦍᦷᧃ kâm jôn, literally "the Tai Yuan language").
* In [Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language") it is known as *kwam yon* (ၵႂၢမ်းယူၼ်း kwáːm jón, literally "the Tai Yuan language").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages") and [Tai peoples](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples")

### Tai migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tai migration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Tai_Migration.svg/250px-Tai_Migration.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tai_Migration.svg)

Map showing the general migration patterns and diversification of the Tai peoples and languages from the original Tai *[Urheimat](/wiki/Urheimat "Urheimat")* of southeastern China.

The ancestors of the [Northern Thai people](/wiki/Northern_Thai_people "Northern Thai people") were speakers of Southwestern Tai dialects that migrated from what is now southeastern China, specifically what is now [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") and northern [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") where the diversity of various Tai languages suggests an *[Urheimat](/wiki/Linguistic_homeland "Linguistic homeland")*. The Southwestern Tai languages began to diverge from the Northern and Central branches of the Tai languages, covered mainly by various [Zhuang languages](/wiki/Zhuang_languages "Zhuang languages"), sometime around 112 AD, but likely completed by the [sixth century](/wiki/Sixth_century "Sixth century").[[5]](#cite_note-migration-5) Due to the influx of [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") soldiers and settlers, the end of the [Chinese occupation](/wiki/Third_Chinese_domination_of_Vietnam "Third Chinese domination of Vietnam") of Vietnam, the fall of [Jiaozhi](/wiki/Jiaozhi "Jiaozhi") and turbulence associated with the decline and fall of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") led some of the Tai peoples speaking Southwestern Tai to flee into Southeast Asia, with the small-scale migration mainly taking place between the [eighth](/wiki/Eighth_century "Eighth century") and [twelfth](/wiki/Twelfth_century "Twelfth century") centuries. The Tais split and followed the major river courses, with the ancestral Northern Thai originating in the Tai migrants that followed the [Mekong River](/wiki/Mekong_River "Mekong River").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Indianized kingdoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Indianized kingdoms")]

Ancestors of the Northern Thai people established [Ngoenyang](/wiki/Ngoenyang "Ngoenyang"), an early kingdom that existed between the 7th to 13th centuries, as well as smaller kingdoms like [Phayao](/wiki/Phayao_Kingdom "Phayao Kingdom"), in what is now modern-day northern Thailand. They settled in areas adjacent to the kingdom of [Hariphunchai](/wiki/Hariphunchai "Hariphunchai"), coming into contact with [Mon-speaking](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language") people whose writing system was eventually adapted for the Northern Thai language as the [Tai Tham script](/wiki/Tai_Tham_script "Tai Tham script").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) In the 13th century, King [Mangrai](/wiki/Mangrai "Mangrai") consolidated control of these territories, establishing the kingdom of [Lan Na](/wiki/Lan_Na "Lan Na"). In the 15th century, King [Tilokkarat](/wiki/Tilokaraj "Tilokaraj") ushered in a golden age for Northern Thai literature, with a profusion of [palm leaf manuscripts](/wiki/Palm-leaf_manuscript "Palm-leaf manuscript") written in Tai Tham, using vernacular Northern Thai and interspersed with Pali and Buddhist Indic vocabulary.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

### Thai subordination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Thai subordination")]

In 1775, [Kawila](/wiki/Kawila "Kawila") of Lampang revolted with Siamese assistance, and captured the city, ending 200 years of Burmese rule. Kawila was installed as the prince of Lampang and Phraya Chaban as the prince of Chiang Mai, both as vassals of Siam. In 1899, Siam annexed the Northern Thai principalities, effectively dissolving their status as sovereign tributary states.

The Compulsory Education Act of 1921 banned schools and temples from using languages other than [Central Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") (standard Thai), in an effort to bring remote regions under Siamese control.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) Northern Thai was relegated from the public sphere, with influential religious leaders like [Khruba Srivichai](/wiki/Khruba_Siwichai "Khruba Siwichai") jailed for using Northern Thai in sermons.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) In the 1940s, authorities promulgated [Thai cultural mandates](/wiki/Thai_cultural_mandates "Thai cultural mandates") that reinforced the importance of learning and using Central Thai as the [prestige language](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

These economic and educational pressures have increased the use of standard Thai to the detriment of other regional languages like Northern Thai.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Sukprasert_2015_153–166-10) Today, Northern Thai is typically [code-switched](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") with standard Thai, especially in more developed and urbanized areas of Northern Thailand, whereas exclusive use of Northern Thai remains prevalent in more remote areas.[[10]](#cite_note-Sukprasert_2015_153–166-10)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Thanajirawat (2018)[[11]](#cite_note-11) classifies Tai Yuan into five major dialect groups based on tonal split and merger patterns. (*See also [Proto-Tai language#Tones](/wiki/Proto-Tai_language#Tones "Proto-Tai language")*)

1. most Tai Yuan varieties in Thailand, Laos and Myanmar
2. [Bokeo Province](/wiki/Bokeo_Province "Bokeo Province"), Laos (A12-34 and BCD123-4 (B4=DL4=DS4))
3. [Mae Chaem District](/wiki/Mae_Chaem_District "Mae Chaem District"), [Chiang Mai Province](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_Province "Chiang Mai Province") and [Laplae District](/wiki/Laplae_District "Laplae District"), [Uttaradit Province](/wiki/Uttaradit_Province "Uttaradit Province"), Thailand (A12-34 and BCD123-4 (A34=B123=DL123))
4. [Tha Pla District](/wiki/Tha_Pla_District "Tha Pla District"), [Uttaradit Province](/wiki/Uttaradit_Province "Uttaradit Province") and [Xayaburi Province](/wiki/Xayaburi_Province "Xayaburi Province"), Laos (A12-34, BDL1234, and CDS123-4)
5. [Ratchaburi Province](/wiki/Ratchaburi_Province "Ratchaburi Province"), Thailand (A12-34 and BCD123-4 (A34=B123=DL123, B4=C4=DL4))

#### Initial consonant clusters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Initial consonant clusters")]

There are two relatively common consonant clusters:

* /kw/ ᨠ᩠ᩅ ᨣ᩠ᩅ (กว)
* /kʰw/ ᨡ᩠ᩅ ᨢ᩠ᩅ ᨥ᩠ᩅ ᨤ᩠ᩅ (ขว, คว)

There are also several other, less frequent clusters recorded,[[12]](#cite_note-14) though apparently in the process of being lost:[[13]](#cite_note-Natnapang_2-15)

* /ŋw/ ᨦ᩠ᩅ ᩉ᩠ᨦ᩠ᩅ (งว)
* /tɕw/ ᨧ᩠ᩅ ᨩ᩠ᩅ (จว)
* /sw/ ᩈ᩠ᩅ ᨪ᩠ᩅ (ซว, สว)
* /tw/ ᨲ᩠ᩅ ᨴ᩠ᩅ (ตว)
* /tʰw/[[14]](#cite_note-16) ᨳ᩠ᩅ (ถว, ทว)
* /nw/ ᨶ᩠ᩅ (นว)
* /ɲw/ ᨿ᩠ᩅ ᩉ᩠ᨿ᩠ᩅ[[15]](#cite_note-17) (ญว, ยว)
* /jw/ ᩀ᩠ᩅ (ยว)
* /lw/ ᩃ᩠ᩅ ᩁ᩠ᩅ ᩉᩖ᩠ᩅ ᩉ᩠ᩃ᩠ᩅ (ลว)
* /ʔw/ ᩋ᩠ᩅ (อว)

#### Final consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Final consonants")]

There are six phonemic [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") in the [Chiang Mai dialect](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_Province "Chiang Mai Province") of Northern Thai: low-rising, low-falling, high-level with glottal closure, mid-level, high-falling, and high-rising.[[18]](#cite_note-theLannaDictionary-21) or low-rising, mid-low, high-falling, mid-high, falling, and high rising-falling[[19]](#cite_note-Gedney1999-22)

#### Contrastive tones in smooth syllables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Contrastive tones in smooth syllables")]

The table below presents six phonemic tones in the Chiang Mai dialects in smooth syllables, i.e. closed syllables ending in [sonorant sounds](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant") such as [m], [n], [ŋ], [w], and [j] and open syllables. Sources have not agreed on the phonetic realization of the six tones in the Chiang Mai dialect. The table presents information based on two sources, one from Gedney (1999)[[19]](#cite_note-Gedney1999-22) and the other one from the Lanna dictionary (2007)[[18]](#cite_note-theLannaDictionary-21) which is a Northern Thai-Thai dictionary. Although published in 1999, Gedney's information about the Chiang Mai dialect is based on data he collected from one speaker in Chiang Mai in 1964 (p. 725). As tones may change within one's lifetime (e.g., Bangkok Thai tones have changed over the past 100 years[[20]](#cite_note-23)), the information about the six tones from Gedney (1999) should be considered with caution.

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Grammar")]

The grammar of Northern Thai is similar to those of other [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages"). The [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") is [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object"), although the subject is often [omitted](/wiki/Pro-drop_language "Pro-drop language"). Just as [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"), Northern Thai pronouns are selected according to the gender and relative status of speaker and audience.

### Adjectives and adverbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Adjectives and adverbs")]

There is no morphological distinction between [adverbs](/wiki/Adverbs "Adverbs") and [adjectives](/wiki/Adjectives "Adjectives"). Many words can be used in either function. They succeed the word which they modify, which may be a noun, verb, or another adjective or adverb.

* ᨾᩯ᩵ᨿᩥ᩠ᨦᨳᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ / แม่ญิงเฒ่า (**mae ying thao**, /mɛ̂ː ɲīŋ tʰa᷇w/) *an old woman*
* ᨾᩯ᩵ᨿᩥ᩠ᨦᨴᩦ᩵ᨳᩮᩢ᩶ᩣᩅᩮᩥ᩠ᨿ / แม่ญิงตี้เฒ่าโวย (**mae nying ti thao woi**, [mɛ̂ː.ɲīŋ.tîː.tʰa᷇w.wōːj]) *a woman who became old quickly*

Because adjectives can be used as complete predicates, many words used to indicate tense in verbs (see Verbs:Aspect below) may be used to describe adjectives.

* ᨡ᩶ᩣᩉᩥ᩠ᩅ / ข้าหิว (**kha hiw**, /kʰa᷇ː hǐw/) *I am hungry.*
* ᨡ᩶ᩣᨧᩢᩉᩥ᩠ᩅ / ข้าจะหิว (**kha cha hiw**, /kʰa᷇ː tɕa.hǐw/) *I will be hungry.*
* ᨡ᩶ᩣᨠ᩵ᩣᩴᩃᩢ᩠ᨦᩉᩥ᩠ᩅ / ข้ากะลังหิว (**kha kalang hiw**, [kʰa᷇ː ka.lāŋ hǐw]) *I am hungry right now.*
* ᨡ᩶ᩣᩉᩥ᩠ᩅᩓ᩠ᩅ / ข้าหิวแล้ว (**kha hiu laew**, [kʰa᷇ː hǐw lɛ́ːw]) *I am already hungry.*

### Verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Verbs")]

[Verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") do not [inflect](/wiki/Inflected_language "Inflected language"). They do not change with person, tense, voice, mood, or number; nor are there any [participles](/wiki/Participles "Participles").

* ᨡ᩶ᩣᨲᩦᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶ / ข้าตี๋เปิ้น (**kha ti poen**, [kʰa᷇ː tǐː pɤ̂n]), *I hit him*.
* ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨲᩦᨡ᩶ᩣ / เปิ้นตี๋ข้า (**poen ti kha**, [pɤ̂n tǐː kʰa᷇ː]), *He hit me*.

The [passive voice](/wiki/Passive_voice "Passive voice") is indicated by the insertion of ᨯᩰᩫ᩠ᨶ / โดน (**don**, [dōːn]) before the verb. For example:

* ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨯᩰᩫ᩠ᨶᨲᩦ / เปิ้นโดนตี๋ (**poen don ti**, [pɤ̂n dōːn tǐː]), *He is hit* or *He got hit*. This describes an action that is out of the receiver's control and, thus, conveys suffering.

To convey the opposite sense, a sense of having an opportunity arrive, ᨯᩱ᩶ / ได้ (*dai*, [da᷇j], can) is used. For example:

* ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨧᩢᨯᩱ᩶ᨸᩱᩋᩯ᩠᩵ᩅᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦᩃᩣ᩠ᩅ / เปิ้นจะได้ไปแอ่วเมืองลาว (**poen cha dai pai aew mueang lao**, [pɤ̂n t͡ɕa.da᷇j pǎj ʔɛ̀w mɯ̄a̯ŋ lāːw]), *He gets to visit Laos.*
* ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨲᩦᨯᩱ᩶ / เปิ้นตี๋ได้ (**poen ti dai**, [pɤ̂n tǐː da᷇j]), *He is/was allowed to hit* or *He is/was able to hit*

[Negation](/wiki/Negation_(rhetoric) "Negation (rhetoric)") is indicated by placing บ่ (*bor*,[bɔ̀ː] or [bàʔ] not) before the verb.

* ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨷᩴ᩵ᨲᩦ / เปิ้นบ่ตี๋, (**poen bor ti**, [pɤ̂n bɔ̀ː tǐː]) *He is not hitting.* or *He not hit.*

[Aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") is conveyed by [aspect markers](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") before or after the verb.

:   [Present](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") can be indicated by ᨠ᩵ᩣᩴᩃᩢ᩠ᨦ / กะลัง (**kalang**, [ka.lāŋ], currently) or ᨠ᩵ᩣᩴᩃᩢ᩠ᨦᩉᩡ / กะลังหะ (**kalangha**, [ka.lāŋ.hà], currently) before the verb for ongoing action (like English -ing form), by ᩀᩪ᩵ / อยู่ (**yu**, [jùː]) after the verb, or by both. For example:

    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨠ᩵ᩣᩴᩃᩢ᩠ᨦᩉᩡᩃᩫ᩠᩵ᨶ / เปิ้นกะลังหะล่น (**poen kalangha lon**, [pɤ̂n ka.lāŋ.hà lôn]), or
    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᩃᩫ᩠᩵ᨶᩀᩪ᩵ / เปิ้นล่นอยู่ (**poen lon yu**, [pɤ̂n lôn jùː]), or
    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨠ᩵ᩣᩴᩃᩢ᩠ᨦᩃᩫ᩠᩵ᨶᩀᩪ᩵ / เปิ้นกะลังหะล่นอยู่ (**poen kalanɡha lon yu**, [pɤ̂n ka.lāŋ.hà lôn jùː]), *He is running.*

:   [Future](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") can be indicated by ᨧᩢ / จะ (*cha*, [t͡ɕǎʔ], will) before the verb or by a time expression indicating the future. For example:

    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨧᩢᩃᩫ᩠᩵ᨶ / เปิ้นจะล่น (**poen cha lon**, [pɤ̂n t͡ɕa.lôn]), *He will run* or *He is going to run.*

:   [Past](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") can be indicated by ᨯᩱ᩶ / ได้ (*dai*, [da᷇j]) before the verb or by a time expression indicating the past. However, ᩓ᩠ᩅ / แล้ว (*laew*,  :[lɛ́ːw], already) is often used to indicate the past aspect by being placed behind the verb. Or, both ได้ and แล้ว are put together to form the past aspect expression. For example:

    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨯᩱ᩶ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶ / เปิ้นได้กิ๋น (**poen dai kin**, [pɤ̂n da᷇j kǐn]), *He ate.*
    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᩓ᩠ᩅ / เปิ้นกิ๋นแล้ว (**poen kin laew**, [pɤ̂n kǐn lɛ́ːw], *He has eaten.*
    * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨯᩱ᩶ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᩓ᩠ᩅ / เปิ้นได้กิ๋นแล้ว (**poen dai kin laew**, [pɤ̂n da᷇j kǐn lɛ́ːw]), *He's already eaten.*

Aspect markers are not required.

:   * ᨡ᩶ᩣᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨴᩦ᩵ᩉᩢ᩠᩶ᨶ / ข้ากิ๋นตี้หั้น (**kha kin tihan**, [kʰa᷇ kǐn tîː.ha᷇n]), *I eat there.*
    * ᨡ᩶ᩣᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨴᩦ᩵ᩉᩢ᩠᩶ᨶᨲᩅᩤ / ข้ากิ๋นตี้หั้นตะวา (**kha kin tihan tawa**, [kʰa᷇ kǐn tîː.ha᷇n ta.wāː]), *I ate there yesterday.*
    * ᨡ᩶ᩣᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨴᩦ᩵ᩉᩢ᩠᩶ᨶᩅᩢ᩠ᨶᨻᩕᩪᨠ / ข้ากิ๋นตี้หั้นวันพูก (**kha kin tihan wanphuk**, [kʰa᷇ kǐn tîː.ha᷇n wān.pʰûːk]), *I'll eat there tomorrow.*

Words that indicate obligation include *at cha* (ᩋᩣ᩠ᨧᨧᩢ / อาจจะ), *na cha* (ᩉ᩠ᨶ᩶ᩣᨧᩢ / หน้าจะ), *khuan cha* (ᨤ᩠ᩅᩁᨧᩢ / ควรจะ)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Northern_Thai_language#Challenged_Word_Khuan_cha "Talk:Northern Thai language")*], and *tong* (ᨲ᩶ᩬᨦ / ต้อง).

* **at cha** (ᩋᩣ᩠ᨧᨧᩢ / อาจจะ, [ʔàːt.t͡ɕǎ]) *Might*

:   * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᩋᩣ᩠ᨧᨧᩢᨾᩣ / เปิ้นอาจจะมา (**poen at cha ma**, /pɤ̂n ʔàːt t͡ɕa.māː/) *He might come.*

* **na cha** (ᩉ᩠ᨶ᩶ᩣᨧᩢ / น่าจะ, [na᷇ː.t͡ɕǎ]) *Likely to*

:   * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᩉ᩠ᨶ᩶ᩣᨧᩢᨾᩣ / เปิ้นน่าจะมา (**poen na cha ma**, /pɤ̂n na᷇ː.t͡ɕa.māː/) *He is likely to come.*

* **khuan cha** (ᨤ᩠ᩅᩁᨧᩢ / ควรจะ, [kʰūan.t͡ɕǎ]) *Should*

:   * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨤ᩠ᩅᩁᨧᩢᨾᩣ / เปิ้นควรจะมา (**poen khuan cha ma**, /pɤ̂n kʰūan.t͡ɕa.māː/) *He should come.*

* **tong** (ᨲᩬ᩶ᨦ / ต้อง, /tɔ᷇ːŋ/) *Must*

:   * ᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨲᩬ᩶ᨦᨾᩣ / เปิ้นต้องมา (**poen tong ma**, /pɤ̂n tɔ᷇ŋ māː/) *He must come.*

Actions that wherein one is busily engaged can be indicated by มัวก่า (*mua ka*, [mūa̯.kàː]).

:   * ᨣᩴ᩵ᨾ᩠ᩅᩫᨣ᩵ᩤᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᩉᩢ᩠᩶ᨶᨶᩰᩬᩡ / ก่อมัวก่ากิ๋นหั้นเนาะ (*kor mua ka kin han nor*, [kɔ̀ mūa̯ kàː kǐn ha᷇n nɔ᷇ʔ]) *(It's that you/he/she) just keeps on eating it like that, you know?*

Words that express one's desire to do something can by indicated by *khai* (ใค่) and *kan* (กั๊น).

* **khai** (ᨣᩕᩲ᩵ / ใค่, /kʰâj/, *to want, to desire*)

:   * ᨡ᩶ᩣᨧᩮᩢ᩶ᩣᨣᩕᩲ᩵ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶ / ข้าเจ้าใค่กิ๋น (**kha.chao khai kin**, [kʰa᷇ː.t͡ɕa᷇w kʰâj kǐn]) *I want to eat.*

* **kan** (ᨣᩢ᩠᩶ᨶ / กั๊น, /kán/, *to try*)

:   * ᨡ᩶ᩣᨧᩮᩢ᩶ᩣᨣᩢ᩠᩶ᨶᨠᩥ᩠ᨶ / ข้าเจ้ากั๊นกิ๋น (**kha.chao kan kin**, [kʰa᷇ː.t͡ɕa᷇w kán kǐn]) *I try to eat.*

**Phor tha wa** (ᨹᩬᩴ᩵ᨵ᩵ᩤᩅ᩵ / ผ่อท่าว่า, [pʰɔ̀ː.tʰâː.wâː]) is used to give the impression or sensation of being something or having a particular quality.

:   * ᨹᩬᩴ᩵ᨵ᩵ᩤᩅ᩵ᩤᨻᩮᩥ᩠᩵ᨶᨻᩖᩥᨠᨾᩣᩓ᩠ᩅ / ผ่อท่าว่าเปิ้นปิ๊กมาแล้ว (**phor tha wa poen pik ma laew**, [pʰɔ̀ː tʰâː wâː pɤ̂n pi᷇k māː lɛ́ːw]) *It seems that he has returned.*

### Final particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Final particles")]

Northern Thai has a number of final particles, which have different functions.

#### Interrogative particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Interrogative particles")]

Some of the most common interrogative particles are **kor** (ᨣᩴ᩵ / ก่อ, [kɔ̀ː]) and **ka** (ᨣᩤ / กา, /kāː/)

* **kor** (ᨣᩴ᩵ / ก่อ, [kɔ̀ː], denoting yes/no question)

:   * ᨾ᩠ᩅ᩵ᩁᨣᩴ᩵ / ม่วนก่อ (**muan kor**, [mûa̯n kɔ̀ː]) *Is it fun?*

* **ka** (ᨣᩤ / กา (and its variants: ก๋า, กา), [kāː], denoting confirmative question)

:   * ᨾ᩠ᩅ᩵ᩁᨣᩤ / ม่วนกา (**muan ka**, [mûa̯n kāː]) *It is fun, right?*

#### Imperative particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Imperative particles")]

Some imperative particles are ᩃᩯ᩵ (แล่), ᨧᩥ᩠᩵ᨾ (จิ่ม), and ᨴᩮᩬᩥᩡ (เตอะ).

**lae** (ᩃᩯ᩵ / แล่, [lɛ̂ː])

:   * ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᩃᩯ᩵ / กิ๋นแล่ (**kin lae**, [kǐn lɛ̂ː]) *Eat! (Authoritative)*.

**chim** (ᨧᩥ᩠᩵ᨾ / จิ่ม, [t͡ɕìm])

:   * ᨡᩬᩴᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨧᩥ᩠᩵ᨾ / ขอกิ๋นจิ่ม (**khor kin chim**, /kʰɔ̌ː kǐn t͡ɕìm/) *May I eat please?*

**hia** (ᩉᩮ᩠ᨿ / เหีย, /hǐa/)

:   * ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᩉᩮ᩠ᨿ / กิ๋นเหีย (**kin hia**, /kǐn hǐa/) *Eat! (because I know it will be beneficial to you)*.

**toe** (ᨴᩮᩬᩥᩡ / เต๊อะ, /tɤ᷇ʔ/)

:   * ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨴᩮᩬᩥᩡ / กิ๋นเต๊อะ (**kin toe**, /kǐn tɤ᷇ʔ/) *Eat, please.*

#### Polite particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Polite particles")]

Polite particles include ᨣᩕᩢ᩠ᨷ (คับ) and ᨧᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ (เจ้า).

* **khap** (ᨣᩕᩢ᩠ᨷ / คับ, /kʰa᷇p/, used by males)

:   * ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨡᩮᩢ᩶ᩣᩓ᩠ᩅᨣᩕᩢ᩠ᨷ / กิ๋นเข้าแล้วคับ (**kin khaw laew khap**, /kǐn kʰa᷇w lɛ́ːw kʰa᷇p/) *I have eaten, sir/ma'am*.

* **chao** (ᨧᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ / เจ้า, /t͡ɕa᷇w/, used by females)

:   * ᨠᩥ᩠ᨶᨡᩮᩢ᩶ᩣᩓ᩠ᩅᨧᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ / กิ๋นเข้าแล้วเจ้า (**kin khaw laew chao**, /kǐn kʰa᷇w lɛ́ːw t͡ɕa᷇w/) *I have eaten, sir/ma'am*.

### Nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Nouns")]

[Nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun") are [uninflected](/wiki/Uninflected_word "Uninflected word") and have no [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"); there are no [articles](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)").

Nouns are neither [singular](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") nor [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural"). Some specific nouns are [reduplicated](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication") to form [collectives](/wiki/Collective_number "Collective number"): ᩃᩡᩋᩬ᩵ᩁ / ละอ่อน (**la-orn**, [la.ʔɔ̀ːn], child) is often repeated as ᩃᩡᩋᩬ᩵ᩁᪧ ละอ่อน ๆ (**la-orn la-orn**, [la.ʔɔ̀ːn la.ʔɔ̀ːn],) to refer to a group of children.

The word ᩉ᩠ᨾᩪ᩵ / หมู่(**mu**, [mùː]) may be used as a prefix of a noun or pronoun as a collective to pluralize or emphasise the following word. (ᩉ᩠ᨾᩪ᩵ᨹᩫ᩠ᨾ / หมู่ผม, **mu phom**, [mùː pʰǒm], *we* (exclusive), masculine; ᩉ᩠ᨾᩪ᩵ᩁᩮᩢᩣ / หมู่เฮา **mu hao**, [mùː hāw], emphasised *we*; ᩉ᩠ᨾᩪ᩵ᩉ᩠ᨾᩣ / หมู่หมา **mu ma**, [mùː mǎː], *(the) dogs*).

Plurals are expressed by adding [classifiers](/wiki/Classifier_(linguistics) "Classifier (linguistics)"), used as [measure words](/wiki/Measure_word "Measure word") (ลักษณนาม), in the form of noun-number-classifier ( ᨣᩕᩪᩉ᩶ᩣᨤᩫ᩠ᨶ / คูห้าคน, "teacher five person" for "five teachers").

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

Pronouns may be omitted once they have already been established in the first sentence, unless the pronoun in the following sentences is different from the first sentence. The pronoun "you" may also be omitted if the speaker is speaking directly to a second person. Moreover, names may replace pronouns, and they can even replace the first person singular pronoun.

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

Northern Thai shares much vocabulary with Standard Thai, especially scientific terms, which draw many prefixes and suffixes from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali"), and it also has its own distinctive words. Just like Thai and Lao, Northern Thai has borrowed many loanwords from [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"), Sanskrit, and Pali.

| [ʔāː.hǎːn] ᩋᩣᩉᩣ᩠ᩁ อาหาร | food | Pali and/or Sanskrit |

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Writing system")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Lanna_lng.png/250px-Lanna_lng.png)](/wiki/File:Lanna_lng.png)

Northern Thai in its own alphabet, the [Tai Tham alphabet](/wiki/Tai_Tham_alphabet "Tai Tham alphabet")

Currently, different scripts are used to write Northern Thai. Northern Thai is traditionally written with the [Tai Tham script](/wiki/Tai_Tham_alphabet "Tai Tham alphabet"), which in Northern Thai is called **tua mueang** (ᨲᩫ᩠ᩅᨾᩮᩬᩥᨦ ตั๋วเมือง /tǔa.mɯ̄aŋ/) or **tua tham** (ᨲᩫ᩠ᩅᨵᩢᨾ᩠ᨾ᩺ ตั๋วธัมม์ /tǔa.tʰām/). However, native speakers are presently illiterate in the traditional script;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] therefore, they instead use the [Thai script](/wiki/Thai_alphabet "Thai alphabet") to write the language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), the [Lao script](/wiki/Lao_alphabet "Lao alphabet") is commonly used to write Northern Thai.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Sign_in_Tai_Tham_and_Thai_King_Kawilorot_Suriyawong.jpg/250px-Sign_in_Tai_Tham_and_Thai_King_Kawilorot_Suriyawong.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sign_in_Tai_Tham_and_Thai_King_Kawilorot_Suriyawong.jpg)

A sign written in Northern Thai, Thai, and English

Some problems arise when the Thai script is used to write Northern Thai. In particular, Standard Thai script cannot transcribe all Northern Thai tones. The two falling tones in Northern Thai correspond to a single falling tone in Thai. Specifically, Northern Thai has two types of falling tones: high-mid falling tone (˥˧) and high-falling tone (˥˩). However, Thai lacks the distinction between the two falling tones, not having a high-falling tone (˥˧). When using Thai script to write Northern Thai tones, the distinction of the two falling tones is lost because Thai script can only indicate a low falling tone (˥˩). As an example, the tonal distinction between /ka᷇ː/ (ก้า (ᨠᩖ᩶ᩣ กล้า) "to be brave") and /kâː/ (ก้า (ᨣ᩵ᩤ ค่า) "value") is lost when written in Thai since as only /kâː/ (ก้า) is permitted. Consequently, the meaning of ก้า is ambiguous as it can mean both "to be brave" and "value". Similarly, /pa᷇ːj/ (ป้าย (ᨸ᩶ᩣ᩠ᨿ ป้าย) "sign") and /pâːj/ (ป้าย (ᨻ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨿ พ่าย) "to lose") have the same problem and only /pâːj/ (ป้าย) is permitted. As a result, the spelling ป้าย is ambiguous because it can mean both "sign" or "to lose". Such tonal mergence ambiguity is avoided when the language is written with the Northern Thai script.

## Northern Thai and Standard Thai

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Northern Thai and Standard Thai")]

The tables below present the differences between Northern Thai and Standard Thai.

### Different sounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Different sounds")]

Unlike Northern Thai, Standard Thai lacks [alveolo-palatal nasal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_nasal "Alveolo-palatal nasal") sound (/ɲ/). Thus, the alveolo-palatal nasal sound (/ɲ/) and the palatal approximant sound (/j/) in Northern Thai both correspond to the palatal approximant sound in Standard Thai:

| [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") | Northern Thai | gloss | note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [jâːk] ยาก | [ɲâːk] ᨿᩣ᩠ᨠ | difficult | cf. [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"): ຍາກ [ɲâːk] |
| [jūŋ] ยุง | [ɲūŋ] ᨿᩩᨦ | mosquito | cf. [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"): ຍຸງ [ɲúŋ] |
| [jāːw] ยาว | [ɲāːw] ᨿᩣ᩠ᩅ | long | cf. [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"): ຍາວ [ɲáːw] |
| [jāː] ยา | [jāː] ᩀᩣ | medicine | cf. [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"): ຢາ [jàː] |
| [jàːk] อยาก | [jàːk] ᩀᩣ᩠ᨠ | desire | cf. [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"): ຢາກ [jȁːk] |
| [jàːŋ] อย่าง | [jàːŋ] ᩀ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨦ | manner, way | cf. [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"): ຢ່າງ [jāːŋ] |

Unlike Northern Thai, Standard Thai lacks a high-mid-falling tone ([˥˧]). The high-mid falling tone ([˥˧]) and high-falling tone ([˦˩]) in Northern Thai both correspond to the falling tone in Standard Thai ([˦˩]).

| [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") | Northern Thai | gloss |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [bâːn] บ้าน | [ba᷇ːn] ᨷ᩶ᩤ᩠ᨶ | village, home |
| [hâː] ห้า | [ha᷇ː] ᩉ᩶ᩣ | five |
| [tɕâw] เจ้า | [tɕa᷇w] ᨧᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ | master, lord, you |
| [lâw] เหล้า | [la᷇w] ᩉᩖᩮᩢ᩶ᩣ | alcohol |
| [lâw] เล่า | [lâw] ᩃᩮᩢ᩵ᩣ | tell (a story) |

#### Aspiration of initial consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Thai_language&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Aspiration of initial consonants")]

Some [aspirated consonants](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant") in the low-class consonant group (อักษรต่ำ /ʔàk.sɔ̌ːn.tàm/) in Standard Thai correspond to [unaspirated sounds](/wiki/Unaspirated_consonant "Unaspirated consonant") in Northern Thai. These sounds include ค, ช, ท, and พ (/kʰ/, /tɕʰ/, /tʰ/, and /pʰ/ respectively), but sounds such as ฅ, คร, ฆ, ฒ, พร, ภ (/kʰ/, /kʰr/, /kʰ/, /tʰ/, /pʰr/, and /pʰ/ respectively) remain aspirated. Such aspirated consonants that are unaspirated in Northern Thai correspond to unaspirated voiced sounds in [Proto-Tai](/wiki/Proto-Tai_language "Proto-Tai language") which are \*ɡ, \*ɟ, \*d, and \*b (ค, ช, ท, and พ respectively).:

| [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") | Northern Thai | gloss | note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [tɕʰīaŋ rāːj] เชียงราย | [tɕīaŋ hāːj] ᨩ᩠ᨿᨦᩁᩣ᩠ᨿ เจียงฮาย | [Chiang Rai city](/wiki/Chiang_Rai "Chiang Rai") and [province](/wiki/Chiang_Rai_Province "Chiang Rai Province") | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦵᦈᧂᦣᦻ [tsêŋ hâːj] "Chiang Rai" |
| [kʰít] คิด | [kɯ́t] ᨣᩧ᩠ᨯ กึ๊ด | think | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦅᦹᧆ [kɯ̄t] "to think" |
| [tɕʰɔ́ːn] ช้อน | [tɕɔ́ːn] ᨩᩬ᩶ᩁ จ๊อน | spoon | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦋᦸᧃᧉ [tsɔ̀n] "spoon" |
| [tɕʰáj] ใช้ | [tɕáj] ᨩᩲ᩶ ใจ๊ | use | cf. [Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): ၸႂ်ႉ [tsa̰ɰ] "to use", [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦺᦋᧉ [tsàj] "to use" |
| [pʰɔ̂ː] พ่อ | [pɔ̂ː] ᨻᩬᩴ᩵ ป้อ | father | cf. [Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): ပေႃႈ [pɔ̄] "father", [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦗᦸᧈ [pɔ̄] "father" |
| [tʰāːŋ] ทาง | [tāːŋ] ᨴᩤ᩠ᨦ ตาง | way | cf. [Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တၢင်း [táːŋ] "way", [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦑᦱᧂ [tâːŋ] "way" |

But not:

| [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") | Northern Thai | gloss | note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [kʰôːt.sā.nāː] โฆษณา | [kʰôːt.sā.nāː] ᨥᩰᩇᨱᩣ โฆษณา | commercial, advertisement | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦷᦆᦉᦓᦱ [xôː.sā.nâː] "advertisement" |
| [pʰāː.sǎː] ภาษา | [pʰāː.sǎː] ᨽᩣᩈᩣ ภาษา | language | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦘᦱᦉᦱ [pʰâː.sáː] "nationality" |
| [wát.tʰā.ná(ʔ).tʰām] วัฒนธรรม | [wa᷇t.tʰā.na᷇(ʔ).tʰām] ᩅᨯ᩠ᨰᨶᨵᨾ᩠ᨾ᩺ วัฒนธัมม์ | culture | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦞᧆᦒᦓᦱᦒᧄ [wāt.tʰā.nā(ʔ).tʰâm] "culture" |
| [tʰām] ธรรม | [tʰām] ᨵᨾ᩠ᨾ᩺ ธัมม์ | [Dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma") | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦒᧄ [tʰâm] "Dharma" |

Though a number of aspirated consonants in Standard Thai often correspond to unaspirated sounds in Northern Thai, when an unaspirated consonant is followed by ร (/r/) the unaspirated consonant becomes aspirated:

| [Standard Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") | Northern Thai | gloss | note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [prā.tʰêːt] ประเทศ | [pʰā.têːt] ᨷᩕᨴᩮ᩠ᩆ ผะเต้ศ | country | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦕᦵᦑᧆ [pʰā.te᷄ːt] "country" |
| [kràːp] กราบ | [kʰàːp] ᨠᩕᩣ᩠ᨷ ขาบ | kowtow, prostrate | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦃᦱᧇ [xa᷄ːp] "to prostrate oneself" |
| [prāː.sàːt] ปราสาท | [pʰǎː.sàːt] ᨷᩕᩤᩈᩣ᩠ᨴ ผาสาท | palace | cf. [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_L%C3%BC_language "Tai Lü language"): ᦕᦱᦉᦱᧆ [pʰáː.sa᷄ːt] "palace" |
